caption: Figure 1. Summary of our approach. While standard image models jointly train an image feature extractor and a linear classifier to predict some label, CLIP jointly trains an image encoder and a text encoder to predict the correct pairings of a batch of (image, text) training examples. At test time the learned text encoder synthesizes a zero-shot linear classifier by embedding the names or descriptions of the target dataset’s classes.
llm_description: This diagram illustrates a zero-shot learning pipeline using contrastive pre-training: First, an image encoder and text encoder are trained on paired image-text data (e.g., "Pepper the aussie pup" with images of dogs). Second, a classifier is built from label text (plane, car, dog, bird) by encoding them into text embeddings. Third, for zero-shot prediction, a new image is encoded, its embedding is compared against all text embeddings, and the highest-scoring text label ("a photo of a dog") is selected as the prediction.

caption: Figure 2. CLIP is much more efficient at zero-shot transfer than our image caption baseline. Although highly expressive, we found that transformer-based language models are relatively weak at zero-shot ImageNet classification. Here, we see that it learns $_ { 3 \mathrm { X } }$ slower than a baseline which predicts a bag-of-words (BoW) encoding of the text (Joulin et al., 2016). Swapping the prediction objective for the contrastive objective of CLIP further improves efficiency another $_ { 4 \mathrm { X } }$ .
llm_description: Zero-shot image accuracy improves with more processed images across three models: the green line (Bag of Words Contrastive, CLIP) achieves highest accuracy and 4X efficiency gain over the orange line (Bag of Words Prediction), while the blue line (Transformer Language Model) shows the slowest growth and lowest accuracy.

caption: Figure 4. Prompt engineering and ensembling improve zeroshot performance. Compared to the baseline of using contextless class names, prompt engineering and ensembling boost zero-shot classification performance by almost 5 points on average across 36 datasets. This improvement is similar to the gain from using 4 times more compute with the baseline zero-shot method but is “free” when amortized over many predictions.
llm_description: A line graph comparing model efficiency and performance: green line (prompt engineering + ensembling) shows higher average scores with significantly greater GFLOPs than the blue line (contextless class names), highlighting a 4x efficiency gain and 5-point score improvement for the larger RN50x64 model.

caption: Figure 5. Zero-shot CLIP is competitive with a fully supervised baseline. Across a 27 dataset eval suite, a zero-shot CLIP classifier outperforms a fully supervised linear classifier fitted on ResNet-50 features on 16 datasets, including ImageNet.
llm_description: Bar chart comparing zero-shot CLIP and linear probe performance on ResNet50 across 20 datasets, showing Δ Score (%)—green bars indicate positive gains for datasets like StanfordCars (+28.9%) and Country211 (+23.2%), while blue bars show negative impacts for datasets like EuroSAT (-37.1%) and KITTI Distance (-34.0%).

caption: Figure 6. Zero-shot CLIP outperforms few-shot linear probes. Zero-shot CLIP matches the average performance of a 4-shot linear classifier trained on the same feature space and nearly matches the best results of a 16-shot linear classifier across publicly available models. For both BiT-M and SimCLRv2, the best performing model is highlighted. Light gray lines are other models in the eval suite. The 20 datasets with at least 16 examples per class were used in this analysis.
llm_description: Average accuracy (%) of six vision models—Linear Probe CLIP, BIT-M (ImageNet-21K), SimCLRv2, ResNet50, Zero-Shot CLIP—plotted against the number of labeled training examples per class, showing performance trends from 0 to 10 examples.

caption: Figure 7. The data efficiency of zero-shot transfer varies widely. Calculating the number of labeled examples per class a linear classifier on the same CLIP feature space requires to match the performance of the zero-shot classifier contextualizes the effectiveness of zero-shot transfer. Values are estimated based on log-linear interpolation of 1, 2, 4, 8, 16-shot and fully supervised results. Performance varies widely from still underperforming a one-shot classifier on two datasets to matching an estimated 184 labeled examples per class.
llm_description: Bar chart showing the number of labeled examples per class needed to match zero-shot performance across 25 datasets, ranging from 184 for FER2013 to 0.9 for EuroSAT and Flowers102, with a mean of 20.8 and median of 5.4.

caption: Figure 8. Zero-shot performance is correlated with linear probe performance but still mostly sub-optimal. Comparing zero-shot and linear probe performance across datasets shows a strong correlation with zero-shot performance mostly shifted 10 to 25 points lower. On only 5 datasets does zero-shot performance approach linear probe performance ( ${ \le } 3$ point difference).
llm_description: Scatter plot showing strong positive correlation (r=0.82) between Zero-Shot CLIP Performance and Linear Probe CLIP Performance across 30+ datasets, with data points clustered along a diagonal trend line and shaded region indicating performance range.

caption: Figure 9. Zero-shot CLIP performance scales smoothly as a function of model compute. Across 39 evals on 36 different datasets, average zero-shot error is well modeled by a log-log linear trend across a 44x range of compute spanning 5 different CLIP models. Lightly shaded lines are performance on individual evals, showing that performance is much more varied despite the smooth overall trend.
llm_description: A line chart plotting model error (%) against model GFLOPs, showing a downward trend where larger models (RN50x4, RN50x16, RN50x64) achieve lower error rates than smaller ones (RN50, RN101), with a shaded confidence band around the trend line.

caption: Figure 10. Linear probe performance of CLIP models in comparison with state-of-the-art computer vision models, including EfficientNet (Tan & Le, 2019; Xie et al., 2020), MoCo (Chen et al., 2020d), Instagram-pretrained ResNeXt models (Mahajan et al., 2018; Touvron et al., 2019), BiT (Kolesnikov et al., 2019), ViT (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020), SimCLRv2 (Chen et al., 2020c), BYOL (Grill et al., 2020), and the original ResNet models (He et al., 2016b). (Left) Scores are averaged over 12 datasets studied by Kornblith et al. (2019). (Right) Scores are averaged over 27 datasets that contain a wider variety of distributions. Dotted lines indicate models fine-tuned or evaluated on images at a higher-resolution than pre-training. See Table 10 for individual scores and Figure 20 for plots for each dataset.
llm_description: Two comparative line charts show average linear probe scores (%) versus forward-pass GFLOPs/image for 27 datasets, illustrating that CLIP-ViT (red stars) and CLIP-ResNet (red stars) consistently achieve the highest performance across both datasets (Kornblith et al.'s 12 and all 27), with RN50x64 and L/14@336px reaching near 90% on the smaller dataset and RN50x64 and L/14@336px achieving ~85% on the larger set.

caption: Figure 11. CLIP’s features outperform the features of the best ImageNet model on a wide variety of datasets. Fitting a linear classifier on CLIP’s features outperforms using the Noisy Student EfficientNet-L2 on 21 out of 27 datasets.
llm_description: Bar chart comparing performance deltas (% change) of Logistic Regression on CLIP versus EfficientNet L2 NS across 23 datasets, with SST2 (+23.6%) leading and ImageNet (-3.0%) lagging significantly.

caption: Figure 12. CLIP’s features are more robust to task shift when compared to models pre-trained on ImageNet. For both dataset splits, the transfer scores of linear probes trained on the representations of CLIP models are higher than other models with similar ImageNet performance. This suggests that the representations of models trained on ImageNet are somewhat overfit to their task.
llm_description: Two scatter plots compare model performance across datasets: left shows linear probe averages over 12 datasets, right over 26. Both axes measure ImageNet score (x) vs Transfer Score (%). Models are color-coded by type (e.g., CLIP-ViT as red stars, EfficientNet variants as blue plus signs), revealing trends like higher ImageNet scores generally correlating with higher transfer scores across both datasets.

caption: Figure 13. Zero-shot CLIP is much more robust to distribution shift than standard ImageNet models. (Left) An ideal robust model (dashed line) performs equally well on the ImageNet distribution and on other natural image distributions. Zero-shot CLIP models shrink this “robustness gap” by up to $7 5 \%$ . Linear fits on logit transformed values are shown with bootstrap estimated $9 5 \%$ confidence intervals. (Right) Visualizing distribution shift for bananas, a class shared across 5 of the 7 natural distribution shift datasets. The performance of the best zero-shot CLIP model, ViT-L $/ 1 4 @ 3 3 6 \mathrm { p x }$ , is compared with a model that has the same performance on the ImageNet validation set, ResNet-101.
llm_description: This chart compares the robustness of different image classification models across seven natural distribution shift datasets. The ideal robust model (y=x, dashed line) shows perfect correlation between accuracy on standard ImageNet and shifted datasets. Zero-Shot CLIP (purple dots) significantly outperforms Standard ImageNet training (blue dots) and existing techniques (grey dots), especially on challenging shifts like ImageNet-R (+51.2%) and ImageNet-A (+74.4%), as demonstrated by the dataset examples showing increasingly distorted banana images.

caption: Figure 14. While supervised adaptation to ImageNet increases ImageNet accuracy by $9 . 2 \%$ , it slightly reduces average robustness. (Left) Customizing zero-shot CLIP to each dataset improves robustness compared to using a single static zero-shot ImageNet classifier and pooling predictions across similar classes as in Taori et al. (2020). CLIP models adapted to ImageNet have similar effective robustness as the best prior ImageNet models. (Right) Details of per dataset changes in accuracy for the two robustness interventions. Adapting to ImageNet increases accuracy on ImageNetV2 noticeably but trades off accuracy on several other distributions. Dataset specific zero-shot classifiers can improve accuracy by a large amount but are limited to only a few datasets that include classes which don’t perfectly align with ImageNet categories.
llm_description: This composite chart compares the performance of various vision models across different adaptation scenarios. The left scatter plot shows how models' accuracy on natural distribution shifts correlates with their accuracy on class-subsampled ImageNet, revealing that robust models (like Adaptive Zero-Shot CLIP) perform better under distribution shift than standard training. The right charts quantify adaptation gains: models adapted to ImageNet see significant accuracy boosts (+9.2% for ImageNet), while those adapted to class shifts (e.g., YouTube-BB) achieve even larger gains (+26.9%), demonstrating superior generalization to new classes compared to zero-shot baselines.

caption: Figure 15. Few-shot CLIP also increases effective robustness compared to existing ImageNet models but is less robust than zero-shot CLIP. Minimizing the amount of ImageNet training data used for adaption increases effective robustness at the cost of decreasing relative robustness. 16-shot logistic regression CLIP matches zero-shot CLIP on ImageNet, as previously reported in Figure 7, but is less robust.
llm_description: This scatter plot compares model performance across seven natural distribution shift datasets (y-axis) against their performance on class-subsampled ImageNet (x-axis). It shows that few-shot CLIP models (red dots) achieve higher average accuracy than standard ImageNet training (blue dots) or robustness interventions (brown dots), with performance improving as the number of shots increases from 1 to 128. The dashed black line represents the ideal robust model (y=x), indicating that most models fall short of perfect alignment between the two metrics.

caption: Figure 16. The hardest problems for CLIP also tend to be the hardest problems for humans. Here we rank image categories by difficulty for CLIP as measured as probability of the correct label.
llm_description: This line chart compares the accuracy (%) of three methods—Zero-Shot CLIP (blue), One-Shot Human (orange), and Zero-Shot Human (green)—across 25 dog breeds. Zero-Shot CLIP consistently outperforms the others, maintaining high accuracy above 80% for most breeds. One-Shot Human accuracy is generally higher than Zero-Shot Human but more volatile. Zero-Shot Human accuracy is lowest and shows the most fluctuation, often dipping below 40%. The performance gap between Zero-Shot CLIP and the human-based methods is largest for breeds like the English Cocker Spaniel and Ragdoll.

caption: Figure 17. Few statistically significant improvements in accuracy due to detected data overlap. (Left) While several datasets have up to $\pm 2 0 \%$ apparent differences in zero-shot accuracy on detected overlapping vs clean examples only 5 datasets out of 35 total have $9 9 . 5 \%$ Clopper-Pearson confidence intervals that exclude a $0 \%$ accuracy difference. 2 of these datasets do worse on overlapping data. (Right) Since the percentage of detected overlapping examples is almost always in the single digits, the overall test accuracy gain due to overlap is much smaller with the largest estimated increase being only $0 . 6 \%$ on Birdsnap. Similarly, for only 6 datasets are the accuracy improvements statistically significant when calculated using a one-sided binomial test.
llm_description: Two scatter plots analyze how detected data overlap (%) affects model accuracy across datasets: left plot shows absolute accuracy difference (with error bars), revealing CIFAR-100 and SUN397 benefit from overlap while Kinetics-700 suffers; right plot shows overall accuracy change due to overlap, with color-coded significance levels (p < 1e-3, p < 0.05, p > 0.05) indicating statistical impact on datasets like CIFAR-100, Birdsnap, and Country211.

caption: Figure 18. CLIP performance on Member of Congress images when given the combined returned label set for the images from Google Cloud Vision, Amazon Rekognition and Microsoft Azure Computer Vision. The 20 most gendered labels for men and women were identified with $\chi ^ { 2 }$ tests with the threshold at $0 . 5 \%$ . Labels are sorted by absolute frequencies. Bars denote the percentage of images for a certain label by gender.
llm_description: Two bar charts compare the most frequent labels assigned to images of women (left, red bars) versus men (right, green bars), revealing stark gender-based biases: women are overwhelmingly labeled with identity terms ("woman," "lady," "female") and appearance descriptors ("blonde," "hot"), while men are dominated by occupation/role labels ("man," "male," "player," "military officer") and physical attributes ("face," "head," "suit").

caption: Figure 19. Two example images from the Rendered SST2 dataset
llm_description: Two contrasting film reviews side by side: one praises the director's energetic, nuanced storytelling and quirky, non-stereotypical cast; the other criticizes the filmmakers for lacking direction and technical skill, resulting in an undetermined narrative destination.

caption: Figure 20. Linear probe performance plotted for each of the 27 datasets, using the data from Table 10.
llm_description: A grid of 25 performance plots comparing 10 different visual models (CLIP-ViT, EfficientNet, ViT, ResNet, etc.) across 25 diverse datasets (Food101, ImageNet, EuroSAT, etc.), showing how each model's accuracy evolves with increasing computational cost (GFLOPs/image) or training data size (log scale), revealing clear performance trade-offs and relative strengths.

caption: Figure 21. Visualization of predictions from 36 CLIP zero-shot classifiers. All examples are random with the exception of reselecting Hateful Memes to avoid offensive content. The predicted probability of the top 5 classes is shown along with the text used to represent the class. When more than one template is used, the first template is shown. The ground truth label is colored green while an incorrect prediction is colored orange.
llm_description: This chart visualizes the performance of a vision model across 29 diverse datasets, measuring its accuracy by showing the correct label's rank and probability among competing text descriptions. Each dataset—ranging from food items and animals to street signs and abstract textures—displays how confidently the model identifies the correct image content based on natural language supervision, with green bars indicating high confidence for the ground-truth label.

caption: Table 11. Zero-shot performance of CLIP models over 27 datasets.
Figure 22. CLIP’s zero-shot performance compared to linear-probe ResNet performance
llm_description: Performance comparison of CLIP-ViT, CLIP-ResNet, and ResNet across 25 datasets, plotting accuracy (or mean per class) vs. computational cost (GFLOPs/image), revealing that CLIP-ViT consistently outperforms the others on most benchmarks while CLIP-ResNet often matches or exceeds ResNet.
